coredata.dc:title: Selective determination of DNA based on the fluorescence recovery of carbon dots quenched by Ru(bpy)2(dppz)2+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Cengceng
Hu, Qi
Wu, Shu
Chen, Fang
coredata.dc:description: A quick, highly selective and sensitive method for the determination of DNA was constructed based on the recovery of red fluorescence of carbon dots quenched by a ruthenium complex (Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, bpy = 2,2′-bipyridine; dppz = dipyrido [3.2-a:2′,3′-c]phenazine). The carbon dots showed two emission peaks at 450 and 683 nm under excitation wavelength of 420 nm. After addition of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, the fluorescence of carbon dots at 683 nm was markedly quenched. Due to the stronger interaction between DNA and Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, the quenched fluorescence of carbon dots was recovered with DNA added. Under the optimum conditions, there was a good linear relationship between the degree of fluorescence recovery of carbon dots and DNA concentration in range of 0.0150–9.60 μg mL−1. The detection limit was 0.00536 μg mL−1. The carbon dots were successfully applied to detect DNA in the simulated sample and the spiked recoveries were between 97.4% and 106%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ru(bpy)2(dppz)2+
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescence recovering
DNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and spectral characterization of CDs
Spectral properties of CDs, Ru(bpy)2(dppz)2+ and DNA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High resolution transmission electron microscopy image of CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs; (C) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs; (D) Infrared spectra of CDs.

(A) Absorption spectra of the (a) CDs solution, (b) Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, (c) CDs- Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, (d) CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+-DNA; (B)Fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs solution, (b) CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, (c) CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+-DNA at 683 nm. (Image of inset: the CDs solution exposed to visible light (420 nm). The concentration of CDs, Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+and DNA was 48.0 μg mL−1, 4.00 μmol L−1 and 9.60 μg mL−1; pH of PBS buffer solution was 5.91.).

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence of (a) CDs and (b) CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ at 683 nm; (B) Effect of concentration of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ on fluorescence recovering of CDs (F: the fluorescence of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+-DNA, F0: the fluorescence of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+; the concentration of CDs, Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ and DNA was 48.0 μg mL−1, 4.00 μmol L−1 and 9.60 μg mL−1; pH of PBS buffer solution was 5.91).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ with the addition of different concentration of DNA (0.0150, 0.030, … …, 8.40, 9.60, 10.8 μg mL−1) in PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.91); (B) The corresponding calibration curve of the fluorescent detection toward DNA (n = 3) with a concentration of 0.0150–9.60 μg mL−1(F: the fluorescence of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+-DNA, F0: the fluorescence of CDs- Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+).
Selectivity (A) and anti-interference (B) of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ in the presence of various substances.
The schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs and the application in the detection of DNA.
Detection for DNA in the simulated sample.

head.ce:title: Selective determination of DNA based on the fluorescence recovery of carbon dots quenched by Ru(bpy)2(dppz)2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic Chemistry & Materia Medica, Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage (HUST), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 430074, Wuhan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic Chemistry & Materia Medica
Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage (HUST)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic Chemistry & Materia Medica, Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage (HUST), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 430074, Wuhan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A quick, highly selective and sensitive method for the determination of DNA was constructed based on the recovery of red fluorescence of carbon dots quenched by a ruthenium complex (Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, bpy = 2,2′-bipyridine; dppz = dipyrido [3.2-a:2′,3′-c]phenazine). The carbon dots showed two emission peaks at 450 and 683 nm under excitation wavelength of 420 nm. After addition of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, the fluorescence of carbon dots at 683 nm was markedly quenched. Due to the stronger interaction between DNA and Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, the quenched fluorescence of carbon dots was recovered with DNA added. Under the optimum conditions, there was a good linear relationship between the degree of fluorescence recovery of carbon dots and DNA concentration in range of 0.0150–9.60 μg mL−1. The detection limit was 0.00536 μg mL−1. The carbon dots were successfully applied to detect DNA in the simulated sample and the spiked recoveries were between 97.4% and 106%.

ce:section.ce:para: As a carrier of gene information, Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is an important material of life inheritance and has the function of storing and transmitting information. Therefore, quantitative analysis of DNA is of great significance for clinical diagnosis, biomedicine, and forensic investigation [1,2]. Traditional methods for determination of DNA included colorimetric methods [3,4], electrochemical sensing [5–7], fluorimetric methods and so on. Among that, fluorimetric methods for detecting DNA based on carbon nanomaterials [8,9], organic molecules [10,11] and metal complexes [12,13] were widely used. However, developing a rapid, costless and highly sensitive strategy has been focused.

Carbon dots (CDs), with green, simple preparation and good optical properties, have been used as a kind of fluorescent probe for analyte detection [14–17], fluorescence imaging [18–20] and drug delivery [21,22]. The CDs prepared by different methods could be applied to selectively recognize Fe3+ [23], Cd2+ [24], Hg2+ [25]. Meanwhile, after adding other substances which have stronger interaction with metal ions, the quenched fluorescence of CDs would get recovered [26–28]. Liu et al. mixed N-acetylcysteine with branched polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon dots that were quenched by copper ion, leading to the fluorescence of the CDs recovered [26]. Huang et al. designed a turn-off/on strategy for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate based on the fluorescence of CDs quenched by ferric ions and recovered by adenosine triphosphate [28]. Yet, there were few reports about that fluorescence of CDs quenched by complexes were used as a fluorescence probe to detect various analytes.

Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, as a molecular “light switches” complex for nucleic acids, is in possession of highly specific and strong bind force with DNA. The bind constant was 106–107 (mol/L)−1 [29]. Based on the fluorescence or light scattering response toward DNA, methods for selectively detecting DNA could be established [30,31]. One of the disadvantages of the “light switches” complexes was that the fluorescence quantum yield was lower than 0.05 [32]. Compared with these “light switches” complexes, CDs have much higher quantum yield. In this work, an efficient and high sensitive “off-on” fluorescent probe was conducted for selectively detection of DNA on basis that fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ which bind stronger with DNA. Firstly, with glutathione and formamide as precursor, carbon dots with red emission wavelength of 683 nm were prepared using a simple solvothermal method. Under pH = 5.91, the fluorescence intensity only at 683 nm can be obviously quenched after adding Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+. Then with the addition of DNA, the quenched fluorescence intensity of CDs was recovered. The schematic diagram of detection principle is shown in Scheme 1 . Because the specific binding between Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ and DNA, this method showed highly selectivity and anti-interference ability for DNA detection.

Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA), LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, USA), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra (Bruker, Germany), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA).

Glutathione, formamide, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, Na2EDTA, MgCl2, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), cysteine, glutamine acid, glycine, bovine serum albumin, histidine, methionine, glucose, ct-DNA, Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ solution (2 × 10−4 mol L−1), the deionized water.
A certain amount of ct-DNA was directly dissolved into 0.05 mol L−1 NaCl solution to obtain a DNA solution of 120 μg mL−1, which was stored at 4 °C.

The CDs was synthesized via a simple one-step solvothermal method, according to relevant literature [33] and further modification. Briefly, 0.302 g glutathione and 10 mL formamide were mixed homogeneously by ultrasonic. The above mixture was transferred into a Teflon reaction kettle followed by solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 1 h to dark green liquid. The CDs solution was separated by filtration and stored at 4 °C. The as-prepared CDs had good solubility and possessed red fluorescence under visible light. Effects of the prepared conditions of CDs were shown in Fig.S1.

DNA was determined based on the recovery of red emission fluorescence of carbon quantum dots quenched by ruthenium complex. The CDs was mixed with certain volume of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ solution in PBS buffer solution, causing the fluorescence of CDs quenched. Next, with the increasing concentration of DNA, the fluorescence of CDs at 683 nm was continuously recovered. Finally, the measured DNA concentration ranged from 0.0150 to 9.60 μg mL−1. The fluorescence intensity was analyzed at excitation wavelength of 420 nm and emission wavelengths of 683 nm. The excitation and emission slits were set as 10 nm and 5 nm. Each standard solution was detected in parallel three times.

To evaluate the feasibility, the “off-on” probe was used for detection of DNA in the simulated sample, containing 10.0 μmol L−1 of metal ions, 9.60 μg mL−1 of amino acid, ATP, BSA, glucose, EDTA. In PBS buffer solution, Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ solution at 4.00 μmol L−1 of concentration was added into CDs solution, which mixed uniformly to obtain stable fluorescence intensity. Continuously, the simulated sample solution was added into the above mixture solution for fluorometric detection. Each sample was measured in parallel three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and spectral characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Morphology of CDs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), as indicated in Fig. 1 . The HR-TEM image showed that as-prepared CDs were spheroidal with an average diameter of about 2 nm, which were distributed uniformly (Fig. 1A). The Spectral properties of CDs were studied via UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As seen from Fig. 1B, there were several strong absorption peaks of CDs at 260 nm, 320 nm, 415 nm and particularly at 600–700 nm that belongs to visible absorption. That might be attributed by the π-π* transition of CC bond, the π-π* and n-π* transition of CO, CN and CS bonds, relatively.

As shown in Fig. 1C, the as-prepared CDs had two obvious emission peaks with the optimal emission/excitation wavelengths of 450 nm/380 nm and 683 nm/420 nm. Thus, the as-prepared CDs might contain the mixture or link of two different fluorescent CDs [34,35]. But the two emission peaks of the CDs belong to one CDs, rather than the mixture of two different fluorescent CDs, which was confirmed by Wang et al. [36]. As we know, the fluorescence mechanism of CDs was generally considered to be generated by carbongenic core state emission which showed excitation-dependence and surface molecular state emission which showed excitation-independence [33]. Different emission centers were existed because of several groups on the surface of CDs and the dominant emission centers will alternate as the excitation wavelength changes, showing excitation-dependence [37]. The as-prepared CDs had excitation-independent emission because the emission position had not changed with excitation wavelength increasing. Thus, the optical properties of the as-prepared CDs might be mainly decided by surface molecular state emission.

The chemical structure of CDs was further investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). As indicated in Fig. 1D, the IR spectrum implied that the existence of functional groups on the surface of the CDs, mainly –COOH, –OH, –NH2, –CONH. For example, bands at 3451.42 cm−1 and 3331.88 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, which indicated the existence of hydroxyl and amino groups. Double peaks at 2885 cm−1 and 2767 cm−1 were proved the presence of an aldehyde group. The peaks at 1688 cm−1 and 1391 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO and C–N groups, suggesting that amide groups were formed on the surface of CDs. Besides, The peaks at 1088 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 were observed, indicating the presence of CS and sulfur bonds that was oxidized. The above results implied that the as-prepared CDs were doped with both nitrogen and sulfur. Also, there could be the higher degree of surface oxidation and carbonization domain in red luminescence CDs than conventional CDs [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral properties of CDs, Ru(bpy)2(dppz)2+ and DNA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A and B represented the UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs- Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ with and without DNA. As shown in Fig. 2A, there were several strong absorption peaks at 285 nm, 370 nm and 445 nm of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ relatively (curve b) and two characteristic absorption at 600–700 nm of CDs solution (curve a). Compared to CDs, hyperchromicity obviously occurred at 370 nm and 445 nm after Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ added into CDs solution. Meanwhile, the two peaks between 600 nm and 700 nm were red shifted (curve c). The results indicated that CD had a significant interaction with Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+. Continuing to add DNA into above solution, there was a hypochromic at 370 nm and 445 nm. Also, the two characteristic peaks between 600 nm and 700 nm were same with free CDs (curve d). It can be seen that the interaction between Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ and DNA was stronger than that of CDs and Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, which was associated with the intrinsic property of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+.

Simultaneously, the annihilated fluorescence of CDs by Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ can be restored obviously with the addition of DNA, as depicted in Fig. 2B, which was consistent with the above phenomenon of UV–vis spectra. From the image of inset, the CDs can emit strong red fluorescence exposed to visible light (420 nm).

To achieve the best performance of the system which was based on the reversion of fluorescence quenching of carbon dots by Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+, several experimental conditions including the concentration of CDs, pH of PBS buffer solution and the concentration of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ were optimized.

The relationship between the FL intensity and concentration of CDs was explored, as seen in Fig. S2. There was a good linear relationship between the FL intensity and concentration of CDs in the range of 12.0–48.0 μg mL−1. Continuing to increase the concentration of CDs, the increase of fluorescence intensity slowed down gradually. This was mainly due to that CDs collided with each other at high concentrations, resulting in self-quenching. So, CDs concentration at 48.0 μg mL−1 was selected for the optimal condition throughout the experiment.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs with and without Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ were influenced by various pH values. As indicated in Fig. 3 A, the FL intensity of CDs was gradually increased varying from 5.29 to 5.91. And the intensity of CDs reached to the maximum value at pH 5.91. With pH continued to be increased, the intensity of CDs was decreased. But the quenched effect of CDs by Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ was decreased while pH increased. The fact might be that the –NH–CO on the surface of CDs was converted to –NC–OH [33]. Thus, the PBS buffer solution at pH 5.91 was chosen as the best reaction medium.

In order to more sensitive detection of DNA, the effects of different concentrations of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ on the reversion of the fluorescence of CDs by DNA were investigated (Fig. 3B). The result indicated that the recovered degree of CDs increased first and then gradually decreased by increasing the Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ concentration. Furthermore, the recovered degree reached to the maximum at 4.00 μmol L−1. As a result, Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ concentration at 4.00 μmol L−1 was chosen for further research.

Under the optimized conditions, the analytical performance of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ were investigated towards DNA standards. As shown in Fig. 4 , the quenched fluorescence of CDs by Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ was restored with the increasing of DNA concentration, and exhibited a good linear relationship between the recovered degree of the fluorescence of CDs and DNA concentration in the dynamic range of 0.0150–9.60 μg mL−1. The linear equation was (F–F0)/F0 = 0.105c+0.0360 (c: μg mL−1, R2 = 0.997, SDslope = 0.162%, SDintercept = 0.547%, F: the fluorescence of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+-DNA, F0: the fluorescence of CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+) (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection was 0.00536 μg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for detecting DNA at concentration of 2.40 μg mL−1 was 2.93%, which was calculated by repeatedly measuring 10 times. Table S1 showed that the comparison of nanomaterial-based fluorescence methods for the detection of DNA. It can be seen that this method was more sensitive than the similar reported results.

To evaluate selectivity and anti-interference ability of the CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ system for determination of target DNA, some possible coexisting substances were tested, such as 10 μmol L−1 of metal ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+), 9.60 μg mL−1 of amino acid, ATP, BSA, glucose, EDTA (Fig. 5 ). It was found that only DNA could significantly restore the fluorescence of CDs, and other coexisting materials did not recover. Taking into consideration, the selectivity and anti-interference of the analytical method were acceptable so that it had a satisfying application potential.

To verify the feasibility and reliability of the above mentioned method for analysis of sample, the CDs-Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ system was employed for the determination of DNA in simulated sample (9.60 μg mL−1 of ATP, BSA, l-cysteine, Glutamic acid, Histidine, Glucose, EDTA and 10 μmol L−1 of Fe3+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+) by using spiked recovery experiment. As shown in Table 1 , the spiked recoveries were between 97.4% and 106%, of which the relative standard deviation were below 5%. Also, each simulated sample was measured in parallel three times. That indicated that the method had applicability of DNA determination in real sample.

In general, this work reported a rapid and efficient fluorescence approach based on the recovery of Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ quenched fluorescence of CDs for detecting DNA in sample solutions. The as-prepared CDs exhibited red luminescence at 683 nm with excitation wavelength of 420 nm, which had higher fluorescence quantum yield than the compounds between ruthenium complex and DNA. By simply mixing, the fluorescence of CDs solution quenched by Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ can be recovered after adding DNA. Due to the selectively strong interaction between Ru (bpy)2 (dppz)2+ and DNA, the probe possessed good selectivity and high anti-interference abilities. The recovery of fluorescence of CDs quenched by the substance that have selective interaction with DNA might be widely used in carbon dots related fluorescence analysis.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Cengceng Zhang: Validation, Writing - original draft. Qi Hu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Shu Wu: Writing - review & editing. Fang Chen: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2019kfyXKJC056) and the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2018QYTD12).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Accurate and sensitive fluorescence detection of DNA based on G-quadruplex hairpin DNA

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, R. Liao, H. Wang, H. Gong, C.Y. Chen, X.M. Chen and C.Q. Cai, Accurate and sensitive fluorescence detection of DNA based on G-quadruplex hairpin DNA, Talanta, 176 (2018) 422-427.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mitochondria-based aircraft carrier enhances in vivo imaging of carbon quantum dots and delivery of anticancer drug
